behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is not at a module boundary — it is whether the AI model has the capability to surface all key terms from legal text. A test that stubs the extractor and checks routing logic would never catch an extractor that misses obligations or mis-identifies parties. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network dependency. LLM-DEP: Correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM extracts from a contract — a stub always returns whatever you hard-code, making the test vacuous. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text disqualifies Agentic. The phrase 'identifies all key terms' is an accuracy/completeness claim about model output, not a routing or schema claim.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real contract containing known parties, dates, obligations, and penalties to the actual clause extractor model. Assert the returned term list includes every expected element. Vary contracts (e.g., one with explicit dates and one with relative dates like 'within 30 days') to probe completeness across formats.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The specific failure mode is the model's ability to reason about indirect and euphemistic phrasing — not about whether the service wires the right model endpoint. LLM-DEP: The qualifier 'even when … indirect or euphemistic' is an explicit claim about AI reasoning quality. A deterministic stub cannot exercise this — a stub that always returns 'high risk' or 'low risk' trivially passes or fails regardless of model quality. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text limits the test to routing, retries, or schema validation. The indirect/euphemistic requirement locks the test to real LLM inference.
behaviors.test_description: Submit contract snippets containing known problematic clauses expressed indirectly (e.g., 'parties may revisit compensation structures' as a euphemism for unilateral pay cuts) to the real risk assessor model under the configured risk policy. Assert each snippet is flagged at the appropriate severity, and that rephrasing to explicit language does not change the flag outcome.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is whether the model has the knowledge and reasoning to evaluate jurisdiction-specific regulations — not a module wiring issue. LLM-DEP: 'Correctly determines' is a judgment-quality claim: a stub that always returns 'compliant' or 'non-compliant' makes the test trivially pass or fail. Regulatory correctness requires the real model to apply jurisdiction-specific knowledge (e.g., GDPR data-transfer clauses for EU jurisdiction vs. CCPA for California). REJECTION-GATE: No text limits this to config loading, request routing, or schema validation.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real compliance checker on a contract with a known compliance gap under a specific jurisdiction (e.g., a GDPR-scoped contract missing a data-processing addendum). Assert the checker returns a non-compliant finding referencing the correct regulation. Also run a compliant variant and assert a clean result, ensuring the model distinguishes the two cases.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments, the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is purely in the merge algorithm — given two structured assessment objects targeting the same clause ID with different verdicts, does the merge output contain both records with their source labels? This is deterministic data-transformation logic. No module handoff is required; the bug would manifest in the merge function itself. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess dependency. LLM-DEP: The merge step does not call an AI model — it combines pre-formed structured outputs. MULTI-STEP: Single deterministic step. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real infrastructure needed. (b) No AI/ML output involved. (c) No agentic chaining. Unit is the minimum real failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two synthetic reviewer output objects for the same clause ID — one with assessment 'standard' attributed to the extractor, one with 'high risk' attributed to the risk assessor. Call the merge function directly with these inputs. Assert the unified report contains exactly two entries for that clause, each retaining its original assessment value and correct attribution label. Also assert no clause entries are silently dropped or overwritten.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure modes here span multiple boundaries: reviewer dispatch, parallel collection, result handoff, and merge. These interactions are not testable by exercising any single module. REAL-INFRA: 'Real contract' signals the need for real inference rather than canned fixtures. LLM-DEP: Each of the three reviewers is an AI model whose outputs must flow correctly into the merge — a stub reviewer always returns a fixed payload, hiding any failure caused by unexpected model output structure. MULTI-STEP: Three independent AI reviewer steps feed a merge step — this is explicitly multiple agentic steps in sequence (parallel fan-out then merge), satisfying the Workflow definition. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text restricts this to a single AI call or a deterministic pipeline; 'three independent AI reviewers … without losing any individual reviewer's findings' requires all three real models to produce findings that survive the full pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run the complete pipeline against a real contract document using all three live reviewer models. Assert: (a) the unified report contains at least one finding attributed to each of the three reviewers; (b) no findings present in any individual reviewer's raw output are absent from the merged report; (c) the pipeline terminates without error. Use a contract with known characteristics (e.g., a clause known to trigger risk and a known compliance gap) so that expected findings are verifiable.
